hook: A workshop-menagerie of giant mechanical beasts in the old shipyards
facts: Built in the disused Nantes shipyards by company La Machine, opened 2007.
Inspired by Jules Verne and Leonardo da Vinci, blending invented machines.
Home base of the 12-metre, 48-tonne Grand Elephant that carries riders.
Founded by Francois Delaroziere and Pierre Orefice.
w: Where ocean liners were once riveted, riveters now build a heron you can fly and a 48-tonne elephant that ambles out spraying the crowd. Half Verne, half da Vinci, all sawdust and grease. The shipyards gone, the city set its engineers to dreaming instead. Steampunk made municipal.

hook: Three-tier merry-go-round of mechanical sea creatures by the Loire
facts: Inaugurated July 2012 beside Les Machines de l'Ile.
Roughly 25 metres high, 22 metres across, three stacked levels.
Levels themed by ocean depth, from abyss to surface, with crab and squid.
Designed by Francois Delaroziere and Pierre Orefice.
w: Not horses but a giant crab, a retro-jet squid and a deep-sea angler, stacked three storeys high in concrete lacework guarded by sixteen fishermen. Riders work the levers themselves, descending from surface to abyss. A fairground built for the bottom of the sea. Climb aboard and crank the tentacles.

hook: Eighteen steel rings strung along the quay, glowing red, green and blue
facts: By Daniel Buren and Patrick Bouchain for the 2007 Estuaire art trail.
Eighteen galvanised rings, 4 metres across, on old quayside bollards.
Aligned over some 800 metres along the Loire by the Hangar a Bananes.
Lit at night, the rings cycle red, green and blue.
w: Eighteen steel hoops bolted to the old mooring bollards where slave ships and banana boats once tied up, framing the Loire like portholes. By day they rhyme with the cranes; by night LEDs wash them red, green and blue. Set here, on this quay, the rings read as more than decoration. Look through, and remember.

hook: Underground passage on the quay confronting Nantes' slave-trade fortune
facts: Opened 2012 beneath a planted promenade on the Loire quay.
A 90-metre passage near the water evokes the hold of a slave ship.
Glass plaques carry names of expeditions and abolition texts.
Nantes was France's largest slave-trade port in the 18th century.
w: Nantes grew rich fitting out slave ships, more than any French port. This memorial buries that reckoning beneath the quay: a low 90-metre passage at the river's edge, glass underfoot etched with expedition names, light filtering as if through a hold. You walk where the city would rather not look. Built into the Loire itself.

hook: Slave-merchant mansions leaning on a vanished island's sandy ground
facts: A former Loire island developed from the 1720s, surrounded by streets after 1926.
Mansions built by negociants-armateurs enriched by the slave trade.
Facades carry carved mascaron masks evoking sea, trade and Africa.
Buildings tilt as the sandy soil settled on oak-lattice foundations.
w: An island no longer, since the Loire's arm was filled in the 1930s, but the slope and the lean remain. Shipowners who made fortunes off the slave trade raised tufa mansions here; carved sea-gods and African faces leer from the lintels. The sandy ground sank and the houses tipped forward, as if bowing. Grandeur off-kilter.

hook: Three-tier 1843 arcade climbing a hillside on an ornate staircase
facts: Built 1840-43, funded by notary-developer Louis Pommeraye.
Three levels linked by a grand wooden-and-metal central staircase.
Statues of the Arts, Sciences and Commerce line the galleries.
Listed historic monument since 1976; featured in films by Jacques Demy.
w: A speculator notary bridged a steep hill with a covered arcade in 1843, three levels stitched by a theatrical staircase that grows more ornate as you climb past statues of Commerce and the Arts. He went broke on it. Gaslit, glassy, much filmed by Demy. Shopping as architecture, vanity as masterpiece.

hook: Belle-epoque biscuit-factory tower, now beacon of an arts centre
facts: LU biscuit works founded 1846; the brand's Petit Beurre dates to 1886.
Advertising tower built 1909 to designs by Auguste Bluysen.
Factory closed; restored from 1998 and reopened as Le Lieu Unique in 2000.
Tower topped by a sculpture of Pheme above zodiac signs.
w: LU made the Petit Beurre by the trainload here, and in 1909 crowned the factory with an absurd advertising tower: a giant biscuit tin under a winged Fame and the zodiac. War wrecked its twin. The ovens cold, the survivor was rebuilt and the works turned arts centre, complete with hammam. Climb it for the rooftops.

n: Cathedral Crypt & Tomb of Francois II
hook: Renaissance ducal tomb and Romanesque crypt beneath the white nave
facts: Tomb of Francois II of Brittany and Marguerite de Foix, carved 1502-07.
Commissioned by their daughter Anne of Brittany, by Michel Colombe.
Four corner figures personify Strength, Prudence, Temperance, Justice.
A 19th-century crypt holds liturgical treasure beneath the choir.
w: Skip the soaring nave and find the black-and-white marble tomb where Anne of Brittany laid her parents to rest. Michel Colombe took five years; the four corner virtues, serene women in stone, are the masterpiece. Below, a crypt keeps the cathedral's older bones and silver. The last duke of an independent Brittany, sleeping in.

hook: Verne's manuscripts and curios in a townhouse above the old port
facts: Opened 1978 for the 150th anniversary of Nantes-born Jules Verne's birth.
Housed in an 1870s bourgeois villa atop the Butte Sainte-Anne.
Holds manuscripts, posters, games and objects from his descendants.
Overlooks the Loire and the former shipyards he watched as a boy.
w: Verne grew up watching ships fit out below this hill, and dreamed up submarines and balloons that outran them. His museum hides in a creaky villa on the Butte Sainte-Anne, crammed with manuscripts, magic-lantern slides and oddments his family kept. A nearby statue keeps him gazing seaward. Round the world from a window.

hook: Riotously tiled 1895 brasserie haunted by the Surrealists
facts: Opened 1 April 1895 by first owner Mrs Calado.
Designed in flamboyant Art Nouveau by ceramicist Emile Libaudiere.
Listed as a historic monument since 1964.
Frequented by Surrealists Jacques Prevert and Andre Breton.
w: A ceramicist let loose in 1895: every surface tiled, mirrored, painted with cicadas and nymphs until the room tips into delirium. Breton and Prevert took coffee under the cherubs. Listed since 1964, it still serves oysters beneath the glory. Belle-epoque excess that never learned restraint, and never needed to.

hook: Botanic garden seeded with a children's-book illustrator's daydreams
facts: Botanical garden beside the train station, roots in the 19th century.
Illustrator Claude Ponti installed playful sculptures from 2014.
Works include a giant napping topiary chick and oversized benches.
Famed for camellias and historic glasshouses.
w: A proper old botanic garden of camellias and iron glasshouses, then the illustrator Claude Ponti turned a few corners into a picture book: a colossal topiary chick asleep on its belly, benches grown to giant scale, a sleepy world for small visitors. Solemn horticulture, gently invaded by whimsy. Mind the napping bird.

hook: Cold War destroyer turned walkable museum on the Loire quay
facts: A 1950s Surcouf-class escort destroyer, 132 metres long.
Decommissioned 1988, moored as a museum ship at Quai de la Fosse.
Listed monument historique since 1991.
Once crewed by 277 sailors and officers.
w: A 132-metre Cold War destroyer tied up where the shipyards once launched her kind, now opened to anyone who'll squeeze through her hatches. Engine rooms, missile decks and cramped bunks for 277 men, kept by veterans who'll tell you how it ran. Listed as a monument, rusting handsomely. Steel proof of what Nantes built.

hook: 1930s covered market on the bones of the city slaughterhouses
facts: Inaugurated 8 January 1937 on the site of the 1829 municipal abattoirs.
Around 150 traders; the busiest market in Nantes.
A rare survival of 1930s reinforced-concrete architecture in the city.
Labelled '20th Century Heritage' in 2014.
w: Where the city once slaughtered its meat, it now buys its oysters, muscadet and butter-cake. The 1937 concrete halls went up on the old abattoir ground, and the stalls have spilled into the surrounding streets ever since. A hundred and fifty traders, listed as heritage. Come hungry on a Saturday and elbow in.

hook: Colour-soaked old fishing village across the Loire from the city
facts: Former fishing village on the Loire's left bank in Reze.
Captains' and cap-hornier houses appeared with 19th-century sea trade.
Houses painted with leftover boat-hull paint, hence the riot of colour.
Reached from Nantes by Navibus across the river.
w: Fishermen here once daubed their cottages with leftover hull paint, and the habit stuck: a maze of lanes in pink, ochre and sea-green, backs turned to the wind off the Loire. Later the sea-captains built taller. Cross by the Navibus and lose yourself between the boats and the hollyhocks. River-borne and gloriously mismatched.

hook: Neoclassical garden promenade ringed by colonnaded townhouses
facts: Laid out from the 1780s by architect Mathurin Crucy on convent gardens.
Sixteen matching neoclassical houses with Ionic columns enclose it.
Statue of General Pierre Cambronne, a Nantes native, unveiled 1848.
Closed by wrought-iron gates at each end.
w: A gated leafy rectangle where Nantes pretends to be a stage set: sixteen near-identical mansions with Ionic columns face off across the lawns, planned by Crucy in the 1780s. At the centre stands Cambronne, the local general best remembered for a one-word reply at Waterloo. Gravel, gates and good manners. Genteel to a fault.

hook: Late-Gothic chapel founded by a duke for his dying first wife
facts: Founded 1469 by Duke Francois II of Brittany at his wife's deathbed wish.
Built from 1470 on rue Malherbe, originally for Saint Anthony of Padua.
Later the Chapel of the Minimes, renamed Immaculee after 1855.
A rare survival of medieval Breton religious building in the city.
w: As Marguerite of Brittany lay dying in 1469, her husband Duke Francois II promised her a chapel, and built this Gothic one off the busy streets of Richebourg. Friars came and went, the name changed twice, war and time wore at it. A quiet medieval pocket most walk straight past. Promised at a deathbed, still standing.

hook: Riverside garden honouring a betrayed Resistance maquis
facts: Small square along the Erdre between the centre and Saint-Donatien.
Named for the Saffre maquis, a key regional Resistance group.
The 1944 Saffre maquis was attacked and many fighters executed.
Bordered by Quai Ceineray and the Erdre waterside.
w: A modest green strip along the Erdre, named for the maquis of Saffre: Resistance fighters who gathered in the woods north of here in 1944, were attacked and overrun, and many shot soon after. Nantes paid for that summer dearly. The river slides past, the plaque stays. Memory tucked beside the water, quietly insistent.
